Pour out your wrath on the nations that don’t know you,
and on the families that don’t call on your name;
for they have devoured Jacob.
Yes, they have devoured him, consumed him,
and have laid waste his habitation.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah confronts the Temple establishment and the people's false confidence that religious ritual could substitute for genuine repentance and justice.

explainer.summary: God reminds Judah of the covenant they broke. Jeremiah discovers that even the men of his own hometown Anathoth are plotting to kill him for his preaching.
explainer.contextInBook: The plot from Anathoth marks the beginning of Jeremiah's intense personal suffering.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To turn around, to return, to repent
wordStudies.meaning: To turn around, to return, to repent. The most important verb in Jeremiah -- God constantly calls His people to turn back to Him.
wordStudies.significance: Shuv captures the essence of repentance: not just feeling sorry but actually changing direction -- turning from sin back toward God.

verses.text: The word that came to Jeremiah from Yahweh, saying,
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: “Hear the words of this covenant, and speak to the men of Judah, and to the inhabitants of Jerusalem;
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: and say to them, Yahweh, the God of Israel says: ‘Cursed is the man who doesn’t hear the words of this covenant,
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: which I commanded your fathers in the day that I brought them out of the land of Egypt, out of the iron furnace,’ saying, ‘Obey my voice, and do them, according to all which I command you; so you shall be my people, and I will be your God;
verses.explainer: Egypt represents the temptation to flee God's discipline rather than endure it -- running from judgment rather than through it.

verses.text:
that I may establish the oath which I swore to your fathers, to give them a land flowing with milk and honey,’ as it is today.”
Then answered I, and said, “Amen, Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Yahweh said to me, “Proclaim all these words in the cities of Judah, and in the streets of Jerusalem, saying, ‘Hear the words of this covenant, and do them.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: For I earnestly protested to your fathers in the day that I brought them up out of the land of Egypt, even to this day, rising early and protesting, saying, “Obey my voice.”
verses.explainer: Egypt represents the temptation to flee God's discipline rather than endure it -- running from judgment rather than through it.

verses.text: Yet they didn’t obey, nor turn their ear, but everyone walked in the stubbornness of their evil heart. Therefore I brought on them all the words of this covenant, which I commanded them to do, but they didn’t do them.’”
verses.explainer: The heart in Jeremiah is the center of human decision-making and loyalty -- the very place where the new covenant will be written.

verses.text: Yahweh said to me, “A conspiracy is found among the men of Judah, and among the inhabitants of Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: They have turned back to the iniquities of their forefathers, who refused to hear my words. They have gone after other gods to serve them. The house of Israel and the house of Judah have broken my covenant which I made with their fathers.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Therefore Yahweh says, ‘Behold, I will bring evil on them, which they will not be able to escape; and they will cry to me, but I will not listen to them.
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula introduces a direct word from God through His prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text: Then the cities of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem will go and cry to the gods to which they offer incense, but they will not save them at all in the time of their trouble.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: For according to the number of your cities are your gods, Judah; and according to the number of the streets of Jerusalem have you set up altars to the shameful thing, even altars to burn incense to Baal.’
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: “Therefore don’t pray for this people. Don’t lift up cry or prayer for them; for I will not hear them in the time that they cry to me because of their trouble.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
What has my beloved to do in my house,
since she has behaved lewdly with many,
and the holy flesh has passed from you?
When you do evil,
then you rejoice.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Yahweh called your name, “A green olive tree,
beautiful with goodly fruit.”
With the noise of a great roar he has kindled fire on it,
and its branches are broken.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: For Yahweh of Armies, who planted you, has pronounced evil against you, because of the evil of the house of Israel and of the house of Judah, which they have done to themselves in provoking me to anger by offering incense to Baal.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Yahweh gave me knowledge of it, and I knew it. Then you showed me their doings.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
But I was like a gentle lamb that is led to the slaughter. I didn’t know that they had devised plans against me, saying,
“Let us destroy the tree with its fruit,
and let us cut him off from the land of the living,
that his name may be no more remembered.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
But, Yahweh of Armies, who judges righteously,
who tests the heart and the mind,
I will see your vengeance on them;
for to you I have revealed my cause.
verses.explainer: The heart in Jeremiah is the center of human decision-making and loyalty -- the very place where the new covenant will be written.

verses.text: “Therefore Yahweh says concerning the men of Anathoth, who seek your life, saying, ‘You shall not prophesy in Yahweh’s name, that you not die by our hand;’
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula introduces a direct word from God through His prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text: therefore Yahweh of Armies says, ‘Behold, I will punish them. The young men will die by the sword. Their sons and their daughters will die by famine.
verses.explainer: Sword, famine, and plague are Jeremiah's recurring trio of judgment -- the standard consequences of prolonged siege warfare.

verses.text: There will be no remnant to them, for I will bring evil on the men of Anathoth, even the year of their visitation.’”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

explainer.summary: Jeremiah complains to God: why do the wicked prosper while the righteous suffer? God's answer: if running with men exhausts you, how will you compete with horses?
explainer.contextInBook: God does not explain the injustice but warns Jeremiah that worse is coming -- not exactly comforting, but bracingly honest.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To turn around, to return, to repent
wordStudies.meaning: To turn around, to return, to repent. The most important verb in Jeremiah -- God constantly calls His people to turn back to Him.
wordStudies.significance: Shuv captures the essence of repentance: not just feeling sorry but actually changing direction -- turning from sin back toward God.

verses.text:
You are righteous, Yahweh,
when I contend with you;
yet I would like to reason the cause with you.
Why does the way of the wicked prosper?
Why are they all at ease who deal very treacherously?
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
You have planted them. Yes, they have taken root.
They grow. Yes, they produce fruit.
You are near in their mouth,
and far from their heart.
verses.explainer: The heart in Jeremiah is the center of human decision-making and loyalty -- the very place where the new covenant will be written.

verses.text:
But you, Yahweh, know me.
You see me, and test my heart toward you.
Pull them out like sheep for the slaughter,
and prepare them for the day of slaughter.
verses.explainer: The heart in Jeremiah is the center of human decision-making and loyalty -- the very place where the new covenant will be written.

verses.text:
How long will the land mourn,
and the herbs of the whole country wither?
Because of the wickedness of those who dwell therein,
the animals and birds are consumed;
because they said,
“He won’t see our latter end.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
“If you have run with the footmen,
and they have wearied you,
then how can you contend with horses?
Though in a land of peace you are secure,
yet how will you do in the pride of the Jordan?
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
For even your brothers, and the house of your father,
even they have dealt treacherously with you!
Even they have cried aloud after you!
Don’t believe them,
though they speak beautiful words to you.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
“I have forsaken my house.
I have cast off my heritage.
I have given the dearly beloved of my soul into the hand of her enemies.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
My heritage has become to me as a lion in the forest.
She has uttered her voice against me.
Therefore I have hated her.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Is my heritage to me as a speckled bird of prey?
Are the birds of prey against her all around?
Go, assemble all the animals of the field.
Bring them to devour.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Many shepherds have destroyed my vineyard.
They have trodden my portion under foot.
They have made my pleasant portion a desolate wilderness.
verses.explainer: The 'shepherds' are Judah's leaders -- kings, priests, and prophets who were supposed to guide the people but instead led them astray.

verses.text:
They have made it a desolation.
It mourns to me, being desolate.
The whole land is made desolate,
because no one cares.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.
Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
They have sown wheat,
and have reaped thorns.
They have exhausted themselves,
and profit nothing.
You will be ashamed of your fruits,
because of Yahweh’s fierce anger.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Yahweh says, “Concerning all my evil neighbors, who touch the inheritance which I have caused my people Israel to inherit: Behold, I will pluck them up from off their land, and will pluck up the house of Judah from among them.
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula introduces a direct word from God through His prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text: It will happen, that after I have plucked them up, I will return and have compassion on them. I will bring them again, every man to his heritage, and every man to his land.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: It will happen, if they will diligently learn the ways of my people, to swear by my name, ‘As Yahweh lives;’ even as they taught my people to swear by Baal; then will they be built up in the middle of my people.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: But if they will not hear, then I will pluck up that nation, plucking up and destroying it,” says Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

explainer.summary: Jeremiah buries a linen belt as a prophetic sign: as the belt becomes ruined, so God will ruin Judah's pride. The people's sin clings to them like a belt around the waist.
explainer.contextInBook: Prophetic sign-acts make abstract messages concrete and visible.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To turn around, to return, to repent
wordStudies.meaning: To turn around, to return, to repent. The most important verb in Jeremiah -- God constantly calls His people to turn back to Him.
wordStudies.significance: Shuv captures the essence of repentance: not just feeling sorry but actually changing direction -- turning from sin back toward God.

verses.text: Yahweh said to me, “Go, and buy yourself a linen belt, and put it on your waist, and don’t put it in water.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: So I bought a belt according to Yahweh’s word, and put it on my waist.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Yahweh’s word came to me the second time, saying,
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: “Take the belt that you have bought, which is on your waist, and arise, go to the Euphrates, and hide it there in a cleft of the rock.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: So I went, and hid it by the Euphrates, as Yahweh commanded me.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: After many days, Yahweh said to me, “Arise, go to the Euphrates, and take the belt from there, which I commanded you to hide there.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Then I went to the Euphrates, and dug, and took the belt from the place where I had hidden it; and behold, the belt was ruined. It was profitable for nothing.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Then Yahweh’s word came to me, saying,
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: “Yahweh says, ‘In this way I, will ruin the pride of Judah, and the great pride of Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula introduces a direct word from God through His prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text: This evil people, who refuse to hear my words, who walk in the stubbornness of their heart, and have gone after other gods to serve them, and to worship them, will even be as this belt, which is profitable for nothing.
verses.explainer: The heart in Jeremiah is the center of human decision-making and loyalty -- the very place where the new covenant will be written.

verses.text: For as the belt clings to the waist of a man, so I have caused the whole house of Israel and the whole house of Judah to cling to me,’ says Yahweh; ‘that they may be to me for a people, for a name, for praise, and for glory; but they would not hear.’
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: “Therefore you shall speak to them this word: ‘Yahweh, the God of Israel says, “Every bottle should be filled with wine.”’ They will tell you, ‘Do we not certainly know that every bottle should be filled with wine?’
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Then tell them, ‘Yahweh says, “Behold, I will fill all the inhabitants of this land, even the kings who sit on David’s throne, the priests, the prophets, and all the inhabitants of Jerusalem, with drunkenness.
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula introduces a direct word from God through His prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text: I will dash them one against another, even the fathers and the sons together,” says Yahweh: “I will not pity, spare, or have compassion, that I should not destroy them.”’”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Hear, and give ear.
Don’t be proud,
for Yahweh has spoken.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Give glory to Yahweh your God,
before he causes darkness,
and before your feet stumble on the dark mountains,
and, while you look for light,
he turns it into the shadow of death,
and makes it deep darkness.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
But if you will not hear it,
my soul will weep in secret for your pride.
My eye will weep bitterly,
and run down with tears,
because Yahweh’s flock has been taken captive.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Say to the king and to the queen mother,
“Humble yourselves.
Sit down, for your crowns have come down,
even the crown of your glory.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
The cities of the South is shut up,
and there is no one to open them.
Judah is carried away captive: all of it.
It is wholly carried away captive.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Lift up your eyes,
and see those who come from the north.
Where is the flock that was given to you,
your beautiful flock?
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
What will you say, when he sets over you as head those whom you have yourself taught to be friends to you?
Won’t sorrows take hold of you, as of a woman in travail?
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
If you say in your heart,
“Why have these things come on me?”
Your skirts are uncovered because of the greatness of your iniquity,
and your heels suffer violence.
verses.explainer: The heart in Jeremiah is the center of human decision-making and loyalty -- the very place where the new covenant will be written.

verses.text:
Can the Ethiopian change his skin,
or the leopard his spots?
Then may you also do good,
who are accustomed to do evil.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
“Therefore I will scatter them,
as the stubble that passes away,
by the wind of the wilderness.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
This is your lot,
the portion measured to you from me,” says Yahweh,
“because you have forgotten me,
and trusted in falsehood.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Therefore will I also uncover your skirts on your face,
and your shame will appear.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
I have seen your abominations, even your adulteries,
and your neighing, the lewdness of your prostitution,
on the hills in the field.
Woe to you, Jerusalem!
You will not be made clean.
How long will it yet be?”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

explainer.summary: A devastating drought hits Judah. Jeremiah prays for the people, but God tells him not to pray for them -- the point of no return has been passed.
explainer.contextInBook: Being told not to pray for the people must have been devastating for Jeremiah, who loved them deeply.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To turn around, to return, to repent
wordStudies.meaning: To turn around, to return, to repent. The most important verb in Jeremiah -- God constantly calls His people to turn back to Him.
wordStudies.significance: Shuv captures the essence of repentance: not just feeling sorry but actually changing direction -- turning from sin back toward God.

verses.text: This is Yahweh’s word that came to Jeremiah concerning the drought.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
“Judah mourns,
and its gates languish.
They sit in black on the ground.
The cry of Jerusalem goes up.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Their nobles send their little ones to the waters.
They come to the cisterns,
and find no water.
They return with their vessels empty.
They are disappointed and confounded,
and cover their heads.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Because of the ground which is cracked,
because no rain has been in the land,
the plowmen are disappointed.
They cover their heads.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Yes, the doe in the field also calves and forsakes her young,
because there is no grass.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
The wild donkeys stand on the bare heights.
They pant for air like jackals.
Their eyes fail,
because there is no vegetation.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Though our iniquities testify against us,
work for your name’s sake, Yahweh;
for our rebellions are many.
We have sinned against you.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
You hope of Israel,
its Savior in the time of trouble,
why should you be as a foreigner in the land,
and as a wayfaring man who turns aside to stay for a night?
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Why should you be like a scared man,
as a mighty man who can’t save?
Yet you, Yahweh, are in the middle of us,
and we are called by your name.
Don’t leave us.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Yahweh says to this people,
“Even so they have loved to wander.
They have not restrained their feet.
Therefore Yahweh does not accept them.
Now he will remember their iniquity,
and punish them for their sins.”
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula introduces a direct word from God through His prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text: Yahweh said to me, “Don’t pray for this people for their good.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: When they fast, I will not hear their cry; and when they offer burnt offering and meal offering, I will not accept them; but I will consume them by the sword, by famine, and by pestilence.”
verses.explainer: Sword, famine, and plague are Jeremiah's recurring trio of judgment -- the standard consequences of prolonged siege warfare.

verses.text: Then I said, “Ah, Lord Yahweh! Behold, the prophets tell them, ‘You will not see the sword, neither will you have famine; but I will give you assured peace in this place.’”
verses.explainer: Sword, famine, and plague are Jeremiah's recurring trio of judgment -- the standard consequences of prolonged siege warfare.

verses.text: Then Yahweh said to me, “The prophets prophesy lies in my name. I didn’t send them. I didn’t command them. I didn’t speak to them. They prophesy to you a lying vision, divination, and a thing of nothing, and the deceit of their own heart.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Therefore Yahweh says concerning the prophets who prophesy in my name, but I didn’t send them, yet they say, ‘Sword and famine will not be in this land.’ Those prophets will be consumed by sword and famine.
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula introduces a direct word from God through His prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text: The people to whom they prophesy will be cast out in the streets of Jerusalem because of the famine and the sword. They will have no one to bury them—them, their wives, their sons, or their daughters, for I will pour their wickedness on them.
verses.explainer: Sword, famine, and plague are Jeremiah's recurring trio of judgment -- the standard consequences of prolonged siege warfare.
Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
If I go out into the field,
then, behold, the slain with the sword!
If I enter into the city,
then, behold, those who are sick with famine!
For both the prophet and the priest go about in the land,
and have no knowledge.’”
verses.explainer: Sword, famine, and plague are Jeremiah's recurring trio of judgment -- the standard consequences of prolonged siege warfare.

verses.text:
Have you utterly rejected Judah?
Has your soul loathed Zion?
Why have you struck us, and there is no healing for us?
We looked for peace, but no good came;
and for a time of healing, and behold, dismay!
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.
Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Do not abhor us, for your name’s sake.
Do not disgrace the throne of your glory.
Remember, and don’t break your covenant with us.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Are there any among the vanities of the nations that can cause rain?
Or can the sky give showers?
Aren’t you he, Yahweh our God?
Therefore we will wait for you;
for you have made all these things.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

explainer.summary: Even if Moses and Samuel interceded, God would not relent. Jeremiah laments: 'Why is my pain unceasing and my wound incurable?' God promises to deliver Jeremiah personally if he repents of his despair.
explainer.contextInBook: Jeremiah's raw honesty with God is one of the most remarkable features of the book.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To turn around, to return, to repent
wordStudies.meaning: To turn around, to return, to repent. The most important verb in Jeremiah -- God constantly calls His people to turn back to Him.
wordStudies.significance: Shuv captures the essence of repentance: not just feeling sorry but actually changing direction -- turning from sin back toward God.

verses.text: Then Yahweh said to me, “Though Moses and Samuel stood before me, yet my mind would not be toward this people. Cast them out of my sight, and let them go out!
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.
The prophetic messenger formula introduces a direct word from God through His prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text: “I will appoint over them four kinds,” says Yahweh: “the sword to kill, the dogs to tear, the birds of the sky, and the animals of the earth, to devour and to destroy.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: I will cause them to be tossed back and forth among all the kingdoms of the earth, because of Manasseh, the son of Hezekiah, king of Judah, for that which he did in Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
For who will have pity on you, Jerusalem?
Who will mourn you?
Who will come to ask of your welfare?
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
You have rejected me,” says Yahweh.
“You have gone backward.
Therefore I have stretched out my hand against you,
and destroyed you.
I am weary of showing compassion.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
I have winnowed them with a fan in the gates of the land.
I have bereaved them of children.
I have destroyed my people.
They didn’t return from their ways.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.
Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
She who has borne seven languishes.
She has given up the spirit.
Her sun has gone down while it was yet day.
She has been disappointed and confounded.
I will deliver their residue to the sword before their enemies,” says Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Woe is me, my mother, that you have borne me, a man of strife,
and a man of contention to the whole earth!
I have not lent, neither have men lent to me;
yet every one of them curses me.
verses.explainer: The 'woe' pronouncement is a funeral cry -- Jeremiah mourns those who are already as good as dead because of their choices.

verses.text:
Yahweh said,
“Most certainly I will strengthen you for good.
Most certainly I will cause the enemy to make supplication to you in the time of evil
and in the time of affliction.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Can one break iron,
even iron from the north, and brass?
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
I will give your substance and your treasures for a plunder without price,
and that for all your sins,
even in all your borders.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.
Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Yahweh, you know.
Remember me, visit me,
and avenge me of my persecutors.
You are patient, so don’t take me away.
Know that for your sake I have suffered reproach.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Your words were found,
and I ate them.
Your words were to me a joy and the rejoicing of my heart,
for I am called by your name, Yahweh, God of Armies.
verses.explainer: The heart in Jeremiah is the center of human decision-making and loyalty -- the very place where the new covenant will be written.

verses.text:
I didn’t sit in the assembly of those who make merry and rejoice.
I sat alone because of your hand,
for you have filled me with indignation.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Why is my pain perpetual,
and my wound incurable,
which refuses to be healed?
Will you indeed be to me as a deceitful brook,
like waters that fail?
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Therefore Yahweh says,
“If you return, then I will bring you again,
that you may stand before me;
and if you take out the precious from the vile,
you will be as my mouth.
They will return to you,
but you will not return to them.
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula introduces a direct word from God through His prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text:
I will make you to this people a fortified bronze wall.
They will fight against you,
but they will not prevail against you;
for I am with you to save you
and to deliver you,” says Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
“I will deliver you out of the hand of the wicked,
and I will redeem you out of the hand of the terrible.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

explainer.summary: God forbids Jeremiah to marry, attend funerals, or go to feasts -- his entire life is a prophetic sign that normal life is ending. Yet God promises future restoration.
explainer.contextInBook: Jeremiah's loneliness was not personal choice but prophetic obedience -- his solitary life embodied the coming desolation.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To turn around, to return, to repent
wordStudies.meaning: To turn around, to return, to repent. The most important verb in Jeremiah -- God constantly calls His people to turn back to Him.
wordStudies.significance: Shuv captures the essence of repentance: not just feeling sorry but actually changing direction -- turning from sin back toward God.

verses.text: Yahweh’s word came also to me, saying,
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: “You shall not take a wife, neither shall you have sons or daughters, in this place.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: For Yahweh says concerning the sons and concerning the daughters who are born in this place, and concerning their mothers who bore them, and concerning their fathers who became their father in this land:
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula introduces a direct word from God through His prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text: “They will die grievous deaths: they will not be lamented, neither will they be buried. They will be as dung on the surface of the ground. They will be consumed by the sword and by famine. Their dead bodies will be food for the birds of the sky and for the animals of the earth.”
verses.explainer: Sword, famine, and plague are Jeremiah's recurring trio of judgment -- the standard consequences of prolonged siege warfare.

verses.text: For Yahweh says, “Don’t enter into the house of mourning. Don’t go to lament. Don’t bemoan them, for I have taken away my peace from this people,” says Yahweh, “even loving kindness and tender mercies.
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula introduces a direct word from God through His prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text: Both great and small will die in this land. They will not be buried. Men won’t lament for them, cut themselves, or make themselves bald for them.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Men won’t break bread for them in mourning, to comfort them for the dead. Men won’t give them the cup of consolation to drink for their father or for their mother.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: “You shall not go into the house of feasting to sit with them, to eat and to drink.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: For Yahweh of Armies, the God of Israel says: “Behold, I will cause to cease out of this place, before your eyes and in your days, the voice of mirth and the voice of gladness, the voice of the bridegroom and the voice of the bride.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: It will happen, when you tell this people all these words, and they ask you, ‘Why has Yahweh pronounced all this great evil against us?’ or ‘What is our iniquity? or ‘What is our sin that we have committed against Yahweh our God?’
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: then you shall tell them, ‘Because your fathers have forsaken me,’ says Yahweh, ‘and have walked after other gods, have served them, have worshiped them, have forsaken me, and have not kept my law.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: You have done evil more than your fathers, for behold, you each walk after the stubbornness of his evil heart, so that you don’t listen to me.
verses.explainer: The heart in Jeremiah is the center of human decision-making and loyalty -- the very place where the new covenant will be written.

verses.text: Therefore will I cast you out of this land into the land that you have not known, neither you nor your fathers. There you will serve other gods day and night, for I will show you no favor.’
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: “Therefore behold, the days come,” says Yahweh, “that it will no more be said, ‘As Yahweh lives, who brought up the children of Israel out of the land of Egypt;’
verses.explainer: Egypt represents the temptation to flee God's discipline rather than endure it -- running from judgment rather than through it.

verses.text: but, ‘As Yahweh lives, who brought up the children of Israel from the land of the north, and from all the countries where he had driven them.’ I will bring them again into their land that I gave to their fathers.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: “Behold, I will send for many fishermen,” says Yahweh, “and they will fish them up. Afterward I will send for many hunters, and they will hunt them from every mountain, from every hill, and out of the clefts of the rocks.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: For my eyes are on all their ways. They are not hidden from my face. Their iniquity isn’t concealed from my eyes.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: First I will recompense their iniquity and their sin double, because they have polluted my land with the carcasses of their detestable things, and have filled my inheritance with their abominations.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Yahweh, my strength, and my stronghold,
and my refuge in the day of affliction,
the nations will come to you from the ends of the earth,
and will say,
“Our fathers have inherited nothing but lies,
vanity and things in which there is no profit.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Should a man make to himself gods,
which yet are no gods?”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
“Therefore behold, I will cause them to know,
this once I will cause them to know my hand and my might.
Then they will know that my name is Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

explainer.summary: Judah's sin is inscribed with an iron pen on the tablet of their heart. Blessed is the one who trusts in the Lord, like a tree planted by water. The heart is deceitful above all things -- who can understand it?
themes: Trust as a tree by water
explainer.contextInBook: The description of the deceitful heart (v.9) is one of the most quoted verses from Jeremiah.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The heart -- the center of thought, will, and deci
wordStudies.meaning: The heart -- the center of thought, will, and decision in Hebrew anthropology. Not primarily emotions but the core of the person.
wordStudies.significance: The deceitful heart (17:9) is the problem the new covenant (31:33) solves: God will write His law on the very organ that was previously corrupt.

verses.text:
“The sin of Judah is written with a pen of iron,
and with the point of a diamond.
It is engraved on the tablet of their heart,
and on the horns of your altars.
verses.explainer: The heart in Jeremiah is the center of human decision-making and loyalty -- the very place where the new covenant will be written.

verses.text:
Even their children remember their altars
and their Asherah poles by the green trees on the high hills.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
My mountain in the field,
I will give your substance and all your treasures for a plunder,
and your high places, because of sin, throughout all your borders.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
You, even of yourself, will discontinue from your heritage that I gave you.
I will cause you to serve your enemies in the land which you don’t know,
for you have kindled a fire in my anger which will burn forever.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Yahweh says:
“Cursed is the man who trusts in man,
relies on strength of flesh,
and whose heart departs from Yahweh.
verses.explainer: 'Cursed is the one who trusts in man, who draws strength from mere flesh and whose heart turns away from the Lord' -- a fundamental choice between human and divine reliance.

verses.text:
For he will be like a bush in the desert,
and will not see when good comes,
but will inhabit the parched places in the wilderness,
an uninhabited salt land.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
“Blessed is the man who trusts in Yahweh,
and whose confidence is in Yahweh.
verses.explainer: 'Blessed is the one who trusts in the Lord, whose confidence is in him' -- the positive counterpart to the curse of trusting in human strength.

verses.text:
For he will be as a tree planted by the waters,
who spreads out its roots by the river,
and will not fear when heat comes,
but its leaf will be green;
and will not be concerned in the year of drought.
It won’t cease from yielding fruit.
verses.explainer: They will be like a tree planted by the water that sends out its roots by the stream -- never anxious about drought because its source is deep and unfailing.

verses.text:
The heart is deceitful above all things
and it is exceedingly corrupt.
Who can know it?
verses.explainer: 'The heart is deceitful above all things and beyond cure. Who can understand it?' One of the Bible's most penetrating diagnoses of the human condition.

crossReferences.connection: Jesus said: 'From within, out of a person's heart, come evil thoughts' -- the same diagnosis of the human heart.
Paul's description of universal human sinfulness draws on this same tradition.

thenVsNow.then: The claim that the human heart is fundamentally self-deceiving challenges modern assumptions about the reliability of following your heart.
thenVsNow.now: The claim that the human heart is fundamentally self-deceiving challenges modern assumptions about the reliability of following your heart.

verses.text:
“I, Yahweh, search the mind.
I try the heart,
even to give every man according to his ways,
according to the fruit of his doings.”
verses.explainer: 'I the Lord search the heart and examine the mind, to reward each person according to their conduct.' Only God can see through the heart's deception.

verses.text:
As the partridge that sits on eggs which she has not laid,
so is he who gets riches, and not by right.
In the middle of his days, they will leave him.
At his end, he will be a fool.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
A glorious throne, set on high from the beginning,
is the place of our sanctuary.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Yahweh, the hope of Israel,
all who forsake you will be disappointed.
Those who depart from me will be written in the earth,
because they have forsaken Yahweh,
the spring of living waters.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.
Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Behold, they tell me,
“Where is Yahweh’s word?
Let it be fulfilled now.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
As for me, I have not hurried from being a shepherd after you.
I haven’t desired the woeful day. You know.
That which came out of my lips was before your face.
verses.explainer: The 'shepherds' are Judah's leaders -- kings, priests, and prophets who were supposed to guide the people but instead led them astray.

verses.text:
Don’t be a terror to me.
You are my refuge in the day of evil.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Let them be disappointed who persecute me,
but let not me be disappointed.
Let them be dismayed,
but don’t let me be dismayed.
Bring on them the day of evil,
and destroy them with double destruction.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Yahweh said this to me: “Go, and stand in the gate of the children of the people, through which the kings of Judah come in and by which they go out, and in all the gates of Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Tell them, ‘Hear Yahweh’s word, you kings of Judah, and all Judah, and all the inhabitants of Jerusalem, that enter in by these gates:
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Yahweh says, “Be careful, and bear no burden on the Sabbath day, nor bring it in by the gates of Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula introduces a direct word from God through His prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text: Don’t carry a burden out of your houses on the Sabbath day. Don’t do any work, but make the Sabbath day holy, as I commanded your fathers.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: But they didn’t listen. They didn’t turn their ear, but made their neck stiff, that they might not hear, and might not receive instruction.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: It will happen, if you diligently listen to me,” says Yahweh, “to bring in no burden through the gates of this city on the Sabbath day, but to make the Sabbath day holy, to do no work therein;
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: then there will enter in by the gates of this city kings and princes sitting on David’s throne, riding in chariots and on horses, they, and their princes, the men of Judah, and the inhabitants of Jerusalem; and this city will remain forever.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: They will come from the cities of Judah, and from the places around Jerusalem, from the land of Benjamin, from the lowland, from the hill country, and from the South, bringing burnt offerings, sacrifices, meal offerings, and frankincense, and bringing sacrifices of thanksgiving, to Yahweh’s house.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: But if you will not listen to me to make the Sabbath day holy, and not to bear a burden and enter in at the gates of Jerusalem on the Sabbath day, then I will kindle a fire in its gates, and it will devour the palaces of Jerusalem. It will not be quenched.”’”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

explainer.summary: God sends Jeremiah to the potter's house: as the potter reshapes clay, so God can reshape nations. If a nation repents, God will relent from disaster. The people plot against Jeremiah.
explainer.contextInBook: The potter metaphor became central to Paul's theology in Romans 9 about God's sovereign right over nations.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To turn around, to return, to repent
wordStudies.meaning: To turn around, to return, to repent. The most important verb in Jeremiah -- God constantly calls His people to turn back to Him.
wordStudies.significance: Shuv captures the essence of repentance: not just feeling sorry but actually changing direction -- turning from sin back toward God.

verses.text: The word which came to Jeremiah from Yahweh, saying,
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: “Arise, and go down to the potter’s house, and there I will cause you to hear my words.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Then I went down to the potter’s house, and behold, he was making something on the wheels.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: When the vessel that he made of the clay was marred in the hand of the potter, he made it again another vessel, as seemed good to the potter to make it.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Then Yahweh’s word came to me, saying,
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: “House of Israel, can’t I do with you as this potter?” says Yahweh. “Behold, as the clay in the potter’s hand, so are you in my hand, house of Israel.
verses.explainer: 'Can I not do with you, Israel, as this potter does? Like clay in the hand of the potter, so are you in my hand.' God retains sovereign authority to reshape nations.

verses.text: At the instant I speak concerning a nation, and concerning a kingdom, to pluck up and to break down and to destroy it;
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: if that nation, concerning which I have spoken, turns from their evil, I will repent of the evil that I thought to do to them.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: At the instant I speak concerning a nation, and concerning a kingdom, to build and to plant it;
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: if they do that which is evil in my sight, that they not obey my voice, then I will repent of the good with which I said I would benefit them.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: “Now therefore, speak to the men of Judah, and to the inhabitants of Jerusalem, saying, ‘Yahweh says: “Behold, I frame evil against you, and devise a plan against you. Everyone return from his evil way now, and amend your ways and your doings.”’
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula introduces a direct word from God through His prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text: But they say, ‘It is in vain; for we will walk after our own plans, and we will each follow the stubbornness of his evil heart.’”
verses.explainer: The heart in Jeremiah is the center of human decision-making and loyalty -- the very place where the new covenant will be written.

verses.text:
Therefore Yahweh says:
“Ask now among the nations,
‘Who has heard such things?’
The virgin of Israel has done a very horrible thing.
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula introduces a direct word from God through His prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text:
Will the snow of Lebanon fail from the rock of the field?
Will the cold waters that flow down from afar be dried up?
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
For my people have forgotten me.
They have burned incense to false gods.
They have been made to stumble in their ways,
in the ancient paths,
to walk in byways, in a way not built up;
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
to make their land an astonishment,
and a perpetual hissing.
Everyone who passes thereby will be astonished,
and shake his head.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
I will scatter them as with an east wind before the enemy.
I will show them the back, and not the face,
in the day of their calamity.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Then they said, “Come, and let us devise plans against Jeremiah; for the law won’t perish from the priest, nor counsel from the wise, nor the word from the prophet. Come, and let us strike him with the tongue, and let us not give heed to any of his words.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Give heed to me, Yahweh,
and listen to the voice of those who contend with me.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Should evil be recompensed for good?
For they have dug a pit for my soul.
Remember how I stood before you to speak good for them,
to turn away your wrath from them.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Therefore deliver up their children to the famine,
and give them over to the power of the sword.
Let their wives become childless, and widows.
Let their men be killed,
and their young men struck by the sword in battle.
verses.explainer: Sword, famine, and plague are Jeremiah's recurring trio of judgment -- the standard consequences of prolonged siege warfare.
Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Yet, Yahweh, you know all their counsel against me to kill me.
Don’t forgive their iniquity.
Don’t blot out their sin from your sight,
Let them be overthrown before you.
Deal with them in the time of your anger.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

explainer.summary: Jeremiah smashes a clay pot at the Valley of Ben Hinnom to symbolize God's irreversible judgment on Jerusalem for child sacrifice and idolatry.
explainer.contextInBook: The valley of Hinnom (Gehenna) later became Jesus's metaphor for hell.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To turn around, to return, to repent
wordStudies.meaning: To turn around, to return, to repent. The most important verb in Jeremiah -- God constantly calls His people to turn back to Him.
wordStudies.significance: Shuv captures the essence of repentance: not just feeling sorry but actually changing direction -- turning from sin back toward God.

verses.text: Thus said Yahweh, “Go, and buy a potter’s earthen bottle, and take some of the elders of the people, and of the elders of the priests;
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: and go out to the valley of the son of Hinnom, which is by the entry of the gate Harsith, and proclaim there the words that I will tell you.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Say, ‘Hear Yahweh’s word, kings of Judah, and inhabitants of Jerusalem: Yahweh of Armies, the God of Israel says, “Behold, I will bring evil on this place, which whoever hears, his ears will tingle.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Because they have forsaken me, and have defiled this place, and have burned incense in it to other gods, that they didn’t know, they and their fathers and the kings of Judah, and have filled this place with the blood of innocents,
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: and have built the high places of Baal, to burn their sons in the fire for burnt offerings to Baal; which I didn’t command, nor speak, which didn’t even enter into my mind:
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: therefore, behold, the days come,” says Yahweh, “that this place will no more be called ‘Topheth’, nor ‘The Valley of the son of Hinnom’, but ‘The valley of Slaughter’.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: “‘“I will make the counsel of Judah and Jerusalem void in this place. I will cause them to fall by the sword before their enemies, and by the hand of those who seek their life. I will give their dead bodies to be food for the birds of the sky and for the animals of the earth.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: I will make this city an astonishment and a hissing. Everyone who passes by it will be astonished and hiss because of all its plagues.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: I will cause them to eat the flesh of their sons and the flesh of their daughters. They will each eat the flesh of his friend in the siege and in the distress, with which their enemies, and those who seek their life, will distress them.”’
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: “Then you shall break the bottle in the sight of the men who go with you,
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: and shall tell them, ‘Yahweh of Armies says: “Even so I will break this people and this city, as one breaks a potter’s vessel, that can’t be made whole again. They will bury in Topheth, until there is no place to bury.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Thus will I do to this place,” says Yahweh, “and to its inhabitants, even making this city as Topheth.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: The houses of Jerusalem, and the houses of the kings of Judah, which are defiled, will be as the place of Topheth, even all the houses on whose roofs they have burned incense to all the army of the sky, and have poured out drink offerings to other gods.”’”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Then Jeremiah came from Topheth, where Yahweh had sent him to prophesy; and he stood in the court of Yahweh’s house, and said to all the people:
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: “Yahweh of Armies, the God of Israel says, ‘Behold, I will bring on this city and on all its towns all the evil that I have pronounced against it, because they have made their neck stiff, that they may not hear my words.’”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

explainer.summary: The priest Pashhur beats Jeremiah and puts him in stocks. Jeremiah's response is one of the most anguished prayers in Scripture: 'Cursed be the day I was born!' Yet he cannot stop prophesying.
explainer.contextInBook: Jeremiah's 'confessions' (20:7-18) are the rawest expressions of prophetic pain in the Bible.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Fire -- used here as a metaphor for God's word bur
wordStudies.meaning: Fire -- used here as a metaphor for God's word burning within the prophet, impossible to contain or suppress.
wordStudies.significance: God's word is like fire in Jeremiah's bones -- even when he tries to stop speaking, the compulsion is overwhelming and irresistible.

verses.text: Now Pashhur, the son of Immer the priest, who was chief officer in Yahweh’s house, heard Jeremiah prophesying these things.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: Then Pashhur struck Jeremiah the prophet, and put him in the stocks that were in the upper gate of Benjamin, which was in Yahweh’s house.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: On the next day, Pashhur released Jeremiah out of the stocks. Then Jeremiah said to him, “Yahweh has not called your name Pashhur, but Magormissabib.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: For Yahweh says, ‘Behold, I will make you a terror to yourself and to all your friends. They will fall by the sword of their enemies, and your eyes will see it. I will give all Judah into the hand of the king of Babylon, and he will carry them captive to Babylon, and will kill them with the sword.
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula introduces a direct word from God through His prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text: Moreover I will give all the riches of this city, and all its gains, and all its precious things, yes, I will give all the treasures of the kings of Judah into the hand of their enemies. They will make them captives, take them, and carry them to Babylon.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text: You, Pashhur, and all who dwell in your house will go into captivity. You will come to Babylon, and there you will die, and there you will be buried, you, and all your friends, to whom you have prophesied falsely.’”
verses.explainer: Babylon is God's chosen instrument of judgment on Judah, and Jeremiah's unpopular message was to submit to Babylonian authority rather than resist.

verses.text:
Yahweh, you have persuaded me, and I was persuaded.
You are stronger than I, and have prevailed.
I have become a laughing-stock all day.
Everyone mocks me.
verses.explainer: 'You deceived me, Lord, and I was deceived; you overpowered me and prevailed.' Jeremiah's most shocking prayer -- accusing God of misleading him about the cost of prophetic ministry.

verses.text:
For as often as I speak, I cry out;
I cry, “Violence and destruction!”
because Yahweh’s word has been made a reproach to me,
and a derision, all day.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
If I say, I will not make mention of him,
or speak any more in his name,
then there is in my heart as it were a burning fire shut up in my bones,
and I am weary with holding it in,
and I can’t.
verses.explainer: 'But if I say I will not mention his word or speak anymore in his name, his word is in my heart like a fire shut up in my bones. I am weary of holding it in -- indeed, I cannot.' Even when Jeremiah tries to stop prophesying, God's word burns within him.

verses.text:
For I have heard the defaming of many,
“Terror on every side!
Denounce, and we will denounce him!”
say all my familiar friends,
those who watch for my fall.
“Perhaps he will be persuaded,
and we will prevail against him,
and we will take our revenge on him.”
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
But Yahweh is with me as an awesome mighty one.
Therefore my persecutors will stumble,
and they won’t prevail.
They will be utterly disappointed,
because they have not dealt wisely,
even with an everlasting dishonor which will never be forgotten.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
But, Yahweh of Armies, who tests the righteous,
who sees the heart and the mind,
let me see your vengeance on them;
for to you I have revealed my cause.
verses.explainer: The heart in Jeremiah is the center of human decision-making and loyalty -- the very place where the new covenant will be written.

verses.text:
Sing to Yahweh!
Praise Yahweh,
for he has delivered the soul of the needy from the hand of evildoers.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Cursed is the day in which I was born.
Don’t let the day in which my mother bore me be blessed.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Cursed is the man who brought news to my father, saying,
“A boy is born to you,” making him very glad.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Let that man be as the cities which Yahweh overthrew,
and didn’t repent.
Let him hear a cry in the morning,
and shouting at noontime;
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
because he didn’t kill me from the womb.
So my mother would have been my grave,
and her womb always great.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

verses.text:
Why did I come out of the womb to see labor and sorrow,
that my days should be consumed with shame?
verses.explainer: Jeremiah's suffering intensifies as his message grows more unpopular. His 'confessions' reveal the personal cost of prophetic obedience.

explainer.summary: During the final siege, King Zedekiah sends to Jeremiah asking for a word from God. The answer: surrender to Babylon or die. God himself is fighting against Jerusalem.
themes: God fighting against His own city
explainer.contextInBook: Telling the king to surrender to the enemy was the most treasonous message a prophet could deliver.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To turn around, to return, to repent
wordStudies.meaning: To turn around, to return, to repent. The most important verb in Jeremiah -- God constantly calls His people to turn back to Him.
wordStudies.significance: Shuv captures the essence of repentance: not just feeling sorry but actually changing direction -- turning from sin back toward God.

verses.text: The word which came to Jeremiah from Yahweh, when king Zedekiah sent to him Pashhur the son of Malchijah, and Zephaniah the son of Maaseiah, the priest, saying,
verses.explainer: Jeremiah delivers God's verdict on kings, prophets, and the nation during the final crisis, while also beginning to point toward restoration.

verses.text: “Please inquire of Yahweh for us; for Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon makes war against us. Perhaps Yahweh will deal with us according to all his wondrous works, that he may withdraw from us.”
verses.explainer: Babylon is God's chosen instrument of judgment on Judah, and Jeremiah's unpopular message was to submit to Babylonian authority rather than resist.

verses.text: Then Jeremiah said to them, “Tell Zedekiah:
verses.explainer: Jeremiah delivers God's verdict on kings, prophets, and the nation during the final crisis, while also beginning to point toward restoration.

verses.text: ‘Yahweh, the God of Israel says, “Behold, I will turn back the weapons of war that are in your hands, with which you fight against the king of Babylon, and against the Chaldeans who besiege you outside the walls; and I will gather them into the middle of this city.
verses.explainer: Babylon is God's chosen instrument of judgment on Judah, and Jeremiah's unpopular message was to submit to Babylonian authority rather than resist.

verses.text: I myself will fight against you with an outstretched hand and with a strong arm, even in anger, in wrath, and in great indignation.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah delivers God's verdict on kings, prophets, and the nation during the final crisis, while also beginning to point toward restoration.

verses.text: I will strike the inhabitants of this city, both man and animal. They will die of a great pestilence.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah delivers God's verdict on kings, prophets, and the nation during the final crisis, while also beginning to point toward restoration.

verses.text: Afterward,” says Yahweh, “I will deliver Zedekiah king of Judah, his servants, and the people, even those who are left in this city from the pestilence, from the sword, and from the famine, into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and into the hand of their enemies, and into the hand of those who seek their life. He will strike them with the edge of the sword. He will not spare them, have pity, or have mercy.”’
verses.explainer: Sword, famine, and plague are Jeremiah's recurring trio of judgment -- the standard consequences of prolonged siege warfare.

verses.text: “You shall say to this people, ‘Yahweh says: “Behold, I set before you the way of life and the way of death.
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula introduces a direct word from God through His prophet Jeremiah.

verses.text: He who remains in this city will die by the sword, by the famine, and by the pestilence; but he who goes out, and passes over to the Chaldeans who besiege you, he will live, and he will escape with his life.
verses.explainer: Sword, famine, and plague are Jeremiah's recurring trio of judgment -- the standard consequences of prolonged siege warfare.

verses.text: For I have set my face on this city for evil, and not for good,” says Yahweh. “It will be given into the hand of the king of Babylon, and he will burn it with fire.”’
